# COMP 110 and its value for natural science students

<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->

## Many natural science students participate in COMP 110

As computer science becomes increasingly integrated in many disciplines, more natural science major tracks are requiring COMP110 as an introductory course to discipline. However, COMP110 does not prominently present ways in which computer science is applicable to these disciplines.

Natural science students may be dissuaded from taking or disinterested in COMP 110 if they are not aware of the applications of its concepts to their future career plans. COMP 110 may benefit from increased engagement from natural science students if they implement readings and exercises revolving around natural science applications of computer science concepts.

## Our analysis

To analyze the opinions of natural science students on the relevance of computer science, we accessed the class survey data of Professor Hinks.

From this data set, we filtered out data originating from 3 highly populated natural science majors from the class: Biology, Chemistry, and Environmental Science.

We sorted these students based on major and graphed their responses to the "valuable" question, which asked students to evaluate:

"Student believes the skills they are learning in this course will be valuable to them in the future. Possible values (1 being Strongly Disagree and 7 being Strongly Agree): 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7"

This was used as a proxy to determine how much value current natural science students attribute to COMP 110 coursework with respect to their future careers.

## Conclusion

We have come to the conclusion that natural science students, more often than not, believe that the topics they are learning in COMP 110 are valuable to their professional futures. Out of the three natural science disciplines we studied, it was found that on average, those students would give the topics studied in COMP 110 around a 5/7 along the valuable variable's scale, indicating that students think there is a moderate amount of value to be extracted from COMP 110. However, there is still room for improvement to make COMP 110 appear even more valuable to natural science students, and including readings that demonstrate ways in which scientists use programming we learn in COMP 110 to help their careers could help boost the valuable variable's results.


There would be some potential costs of uploading readings to help make COMP 110 seem more valuable. For one, members of the COMP 110 team would have to find articles/papers that  show ways in which scientists are using COMP 110 concepts. Searching for said documents would take time out of the team's day, thus making it possible that the COMP 110 team has less availability to meet with students via office hours. Furthermore, if readings are only centered around research in the natural sciences, certain students who wish to apply COMP 110 topics to other professional careers might feel as if COMP 110 only has value for researchers. For instance, if an aspiring game developer only read ways in which COMP 110 helped chemistry students with their lab work, that student may feel as if the entire course is trying to get them to become a scientist and that if they wanted to become a game developer (or any other non-academic professional), they should’ve taken another course to learn computer science.


As for how this idea can be refined to improve future classes, instead of only publishing readings that benefit natural science students who aspire to do research or any other academic work, it could be possible for the COMP 110 team to share readings that show the value of COMP 110 topics across all fields. For example, one week it could be shown how learning computer science can help STEM majors, such as physics, with their line of work, and then the next week can show how a knowledge of COMP 110 topics can help humanities majors such as political science or dramatic arts majors. This refinement still possesses that problem of taking time out of the COMP 110’s day to find said articles, which is why the team can ask UNC professors to provide a written summary of why topics found in COMP 110 matter in their field. This refinement makes it so that COMP 110 doesn’t have to search the internet constantly to find new articles, and having UNC professors share their stories gives first-hand, relatable perspectives of COMP 110’s use in careers, allowing students to more easily appreciate the value of what they’re learning in COMP 110.
